1:
The opening — "as Jesus passed by, he saw a man which was blind from his birth" — is read by the witnesses in close connection with the violence that has just driven Christ from the temple (John 8:59). The grammatical-historical commentators fix the circumstances. Ellicott ties the verse directly to the preceding flight — it was "as He was leaving the Temple to escape the fury of His enemies" that His eye fell on the beggar, the sabbath of the long discourse "now drawing to its close." Robertson notes the rare participle "as he passed by" and disputes Westcott's placing of the scene at the feast of dedication, which comes three months later (John 10:22); he marks too that this is the lone case in the New Testament of congenital blindness healed, a sign Christ named to the Baptist as Messianic (Matthew 11:5).

The Latin and Antiochene fathers gathered in the Catena read the moment as deliberate mercy. Chrysostom finds that Christ, rejected for the depth of His words, "went out of the temple, and healed the blind man," that His very absence might "appease their fury" and the miracle "soften their hard hearts"; He "first saw the blind man," seeking him out rather than being sought. Augustine, opening his long tractate, warns that the whole narrative is so freighted with meaning that "the day would be insufficient" to expound it fully.

The Reformed Calvin sets the key for the chapter: the Evangelist mingles doctrine with the miracle "to point out the fruit" of it, and the blindness "brought from his mother's womb," incurable "by human remedies," gives "an additional display of the power of Christ." The Puritan Henry draws the pastoral note from the same first words — Christ "took notice of the piteous case" of one who could not see Him — and the Wesleyan Clarke supplies the chapter's outline, from the disciples' question through the cure, the interrogations, the excommunication, and the final self-revelation of Christ.

The witnesses thus set the miracle at the very seam between rejection and mercy: driven from the temple by men who would not see, Christ turns at once to a man who cannot see, and makes him the next "manifestation of His power." Robertson's grammatical care — that this is the lone congenital case in the Gospels — and Calvin's doctrinal frame, that the cure is wrought into a sermon on spiritual light, together prepare the reader for a chapter in which physical and spiritual sight will be played against each other to the end. The beggar, unnamed and unasked, becomes the canvas on which the works of God are displayed.
2:
The disciples' question — "who did sin, this man, or his parents, that he was born blind?" — draws the witnesses to the Jewish assumptions behind it, and especially to the puzzle of how one born blind could be punished for his own sin. The grammatical-historical Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic background: it was "a received doctrine in the Jewish schools, that children, according to some wickedness of their parents, were born... maimed," a teaching used to keep parents observant. Barnes adds that it was "a universal opinion among the Jews that calamities... were the effects of sin" (Luke 13:1–4), and that the disciples could not tell whether the fault lay with the man or his parents. Robertson notes the second aorist "who did sin?" and that it is not even clear the disciples expected a cure; they are simply "puzzled" by the theological riddle.

Several voices detect behind the question the doctrine of a pre-existent sin. The Wesleyan Clarke judges that "the doctrine of the transmigration of souls" — that souls are "sent into other bodies for the punishment of some sin... in a pre-existent state" — was "an article in the creed of the Pharisees" and underlies the disciples' words; Wesley agrees they supposed "he might have sinned in a pre-existent state," though "without any ground from Scripture." Barnes likewise cites the belief in transmigration.

The Reformed Calvin uses the question to expose our habitual errors in reading suffering. Since Scripture teaches that "all the sufferings... proceed from sin," we rightly trace affliction to God's hand — "but here we commonly err in three ways": in censuring others "with extreme bitterness," in condemning all whom God afflicts, and in failing to see that God may chasten the innocent. The disciples' instinct to bind this man's blindness to a particular sin is exactly the error Christ will correct.

Ellicott adds that the disciples "noticed that He looked at the man, and it may be that He halted" as He passed, their attention drawn to the sufferer and, with suffering, to the thought of sin; the question was one "discussed in Rabbinic schools." Robertson compares the two cases of lameness from birth in Acts 3:2 and 14:8, and reckons it "not clear that the disciples expected Jesus to heal this case" at all; their interest is theological, not therapeutic. The riddle they pose — guilt before birth, or inherited guilt — is one the whole ancient world felt, and which Christ will neither solve on their terms nor leave unanswered.
3:
Christ's answer — "neither hath this man sinned, nor his parents, but that the works of God should be made manifest in him" — is read with one accord as redirecting the question from blame to purpose, with care taken not to make Him deny all sin whatever. The grammatical-historical commentators insist the denial is bounded by the question. Ellicott: the answer must be "understood with the limitation of the question, 'that he was born blind'" — neither "his special sin nor theirs was the cause." Robertson presses the construction: Jesus "denies both alternatives, and puts God's purpose... as the true solution," conceding that disease is sometimes the result of personal sin (as in John 5:14) and that parents may hand on its effects, yet here is a case "free from blame," and "there is comfort" in it. Barnes agrees that Jesus does not affirm man or parents sinless, only that the blindness "is not the effect of sin."

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine he had announced at v.2: Christ "does not absolutely say" the man and his parents were faultless, "but he declares that we ought not to seek the cause of the blindness in sin," for "God has sometimes another object in view than to punish the sins of men." When the causes of affliction are hidden, "we ought to restrain curiosity."

The Wesleyan Clarke turns the purpose forward — the blindness "shall now become the instrument of salvation to his soul, edification to others, and glory to God" — and Wesley sums the scope of the reply: "It was neither for any sins of his own, nor yet of his parents; but that the power of God might be displayed." The affliction, on this reading, is not a verdict on the past but a stage for the works of God.

Robertson draws the pastoral comfort the verse holds: while "it is sometimes true that disease is the result of personal sin" (as in John 5:14) and parents may "hand on the effects of sin," here is a case "free from blame," so the afflicted need not always read their suffering as a verdict. Gill is careful to add that both man and parents were not without "original sin" and actual transgression — Christ's answer is "agreeable to the design of the question," denying only that a particular sin caused the blindness. The thrust of the verse, for all the schools, is forward: this darkness exists for a coming display of God's works.
4:
The saying "I must work the works of him that sent me, while it is day" opens a real textual question among the witnesses, even as they agree on its urgency. The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott prefer the reading "we must work": Robertson calls it "undoubtedly the correct text," by which "Jesus associates us with him in the task committed to him by the Father," and rejects the variant "I." Ellicott agrees the better reading is "we," Christ "identify[ing] the disciples with Himself in the redemptive work of His mission."

On the imagery of "day" and the coming "night," the schools converge. The Reformed Calvin borrows the figure "from the ordinary custom of life" — "man goeth forth to his work... till the evening" (Psalm 104:22–23) — and reads the "day" as "the time which the Father had fixed" for Christ's working. Barnes notes this was a sabbath (v.14), and that though such a cure had once endangered His life (John 5:1–16), Christ knew it was the Father's will "that he should do good." Clarke marks that it was "about six months" before the crucifixion, the literal evening perhaps drawing on.

The Wesleyan Wesley extends the metaphor to every soul: "when the light is withdrawn night comes, when no man can work" — "no man can do any thing toward working out his salvation after this life is ended" — yet adds the qualification that distinguishes the Master from His servants: "Christ can work always. But he was not to work upon earth, only during the day... appointed for him." The necessity ("I must") the witnesses leave, with Robertson, beyond full fathoming.

Ellicott draws out the force of the better reading "we must work": there stands "before them a striking instance" of the Father's work, and Christ "identifies the disciples with Himself" in the labour of His mission, even as the night of His departure draws near. Calvin grounds the urgency in the rhythm of creation — "the sun riseth; man goeth forth to his work... till the evening" (Psalm 104:22–23) — and the "day" as the Father's appointed span. Robertson confesses we "are not able to fathom the depth of the necessity" in Christ's "I must," a divine constraint laid on the Son's earthly course that the witnesses leave reverently unexplained.
5:
The declaration "As long as I am in the world, I am the light of the world" is read by the witnesses as both the interpretive key to the coming miracle and an echo of the temple discourse just past (John 8:12). The grammatical-historical Ellicott renders it "when I am in the world," the thought being that the two things "necessarily co-exist" — "the true Light cannot be in the world without shining in its darkness" (John 1:5). Robertson parses the indefinite clause "whenever I am in the world," and distinguishes the historic Incarnation here in view from the truth that the Son "as God's Son is always the Light of the World" (John 1:4). Barnes rises to the sublimity of the image: Christ "occupied the same place, filled the same space, shed his beams as far, in the moral world, as the sun does on natural objects."

The Wesleyan voices keep the practical edge. Clarke: "Like the sun, it is my business to dispense light and heat every where," neglecting no opportunity to "enlighten and save the bodies and souls of men" (cf. John 8:12). Wesley: "I teach men inwardly by my Spirit, and outwardly by my preaching... and I show them, by my example, how they must do it."

The Reformed Calvin reads the words as spoken "by way of anticipation," guarding against the thought that Christ's limited "day" of working meant the night might overtake Him "by surprise, as it does on other men." Even while He marks His time as bounded, He is the sun that "illuminates the whole earth by its brightness" — the about-to-be-given physical sight of the beggar standing as the visible sign of the spiritual light He is.

Calvin guards the saying from misunderstanding: Christ speaks "by way of anticipation," lest His bounded "day" of working seem to mean the night might surprise Him "as it does on other men"; even while distinguishing Himself from us He owns that His working time is "limited." Robertson extends the reference beyond the Incarnation to "any previous visitations in the time of the patriarchs, prophets," the Son being "always the Light of the World" (John 1:4). Gill notes the span of that earthly day — "now two or three and thirty years" — soon to close. The coming cure of the man's eyes is, on every reading, the enacted parable of the light that cannot be present without shining.
6:
The strange means of the cure — Christ "spat on the ground, and made clay of the spittle, and... anointed the eyes of the blind man" — fixes the witnesses on why such a method, and what it signifies. The grammatical-historical commentators note the cultural background and its sabbath twist. Lightfoot and Barnes record that the Jews held spittle "medicinal to the eyes," yet expressly "forbade the use of medicines on the Sabbath," so the very remedy compounded the offense; Robertson agrees the "curative effects of saliva are held in many places," but that "Jesus was not asked to cure this man," and we need not infer the cure lay in the clay. Clarke insists the clay "had no tendency to assist the miracle" — no natural means could restore sight to one born blind — so the act was sign, not surgery. Chrysostom, opening on the duty to "search" the Scriptures, finds even "small portion[s] of the words" laden with "hidden meaning."

The Reformed Calvin reads the absurd-seeming means as a deliberate trial: by daubing clay on sightless eyes Christ "in some respects doubles the" blindness, so that one might think He "was mocking the wretched man"; the design was "to try the faith and obedience" of the man, who, "relying on a bare word," obeyed. It is "no ordinary proof of faith."

The Wesleyan Wesley draws the theological lesson from the unlikely instrument: the clay "might almost have blinded a man that had sight" — what could it do toward curing the blind? — and so it "reminds us that God is no farther from the event, when he works either with, or without means," all creatures being "only that which his almighty operation makes them."

Chrysostom, opening on the duty to "search" the Scriptures word by word, finds in even small details "much hidden meaning," and so will not pass the clay by as incidental. Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic lore that "spittle was accounted wholesome for weak eyes," which only sharpens the sabbath offense. Ellicott regards the details of this and the next verse as "part of the sign," impressed on the eyewitnesses and recorded "as such for us," their meaning to be sought rather than dismissed. The witnesses thus hold together the literal absurdity of the means — clay on sightless eyes — and its evident purpose: to test obedience and to fix every eye on the One whose bare word does the work.
7:
The command "Go, wash in the pool of Siloam (which is by interpretation, Sent)" draws the witnesses to the meaning of the name and the testing of obedience. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the gloss. Lightfoot explains that the spring discharged into two pools, and that the Evangelist added the parenthesis "on purpose to distinguish" which — the pool of Siloah, which "plainly and properly signifies Sent" (cf. Nehemiah 3:15). Barnes derives the name from "the Hebrew verb to send," perhaps "because it was regarded as a blessing sent... by God," and stresses that "the waters had no efficacy" in themselves; Christ sent the man "to test his obedience." Robertson parses the aorist imperative "wash," granting that bathing the eyes is good for them while denying the cure was "due to the use of the clay or to the washing."

The Reformed Calvin makes the same point doctrinally: there was no "power or fitness for curing the eyes" in clay or pool, but Christ "freely made use of those outward symbols... to accustom believers to the use of signs," and to show "that all things were at his disposal." The name Sent, on the patristic and later reading, points beyond the pool to Christ the One sent by the Father.

The Wesleyan Wesley adds a pastoral motive: perhaps "our Lord intended to make the miracle more taken notice of," since a crowd would gather to "observe the event of so strange a prescription," and the man's guide, leading him across the city, would publish his errand. What looks like a detour becomes the means by which the cure is witnessed — and, the witnesses agree, the measure of the man's ready obedience to "a bare word."

The patristic and later readers press the name Sent into theology: the pool Siloam, "by interpretation, Sent," becomes a type of Christ Himself, the One sent by the Father, so that the man is healed only when washed in the Sent. Clarke locates the spring "under the walls of Jerusalem," its waters gathered into a great reservoir, and Calvin reaffirms that there was "no power or fitness for curing the eyes" in it, the symbol serving "to accustom believers to the use of signs." Across the schools the lesson is one: the cure waits upon obedience to a word that carries no visible promise, and the man who goes and washes returns seeing.
8:
The neighbours' astonished question — "Is not this he that sat and begged?" — turns the witnesses to the publicity of the cure and the textual detail that secures it. The grammatical-historical commentators note a manuscript variant: the better reading, say Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson, is that they had seen him "that he was a beggar" rather than merely "that he was blind" — Robertson taking the late word for beggar and the present participle of those "who used to observe him." Either way, Barnes observes, the case "could be no imposture, as he was generally known," being perhaps "the only blind beggar in Jerusalem," a man "likely to attract attention." Lightfoot adds the colour of the beggar's customary cry, the appeal to passers-by to "do yourself good by me."

The Antiochene and Latin fathers in the Catena read the choice of so lowly a patient as divine condescension. Chrysostom marvels at the "clemency and condescension of God" that "even the beggars He heals with so great considerateness," making not "the great, illustrious, and noble, but the poorest and meanest, the objects of His providence," and so "stopping the mouths of the Jews," since He had come "for the salvation of all."

The Reformed Calvin notes that the man, "wont to sit and beg at the gate of the temple," was known to all, which "contributed to make many people acquainted with the fame of the miracle"; yet "impiety is ingenious in obscuring the works of God," and so some denied it was even the same man. The Puritan Henry treats the whole street-debate as providential confirmation: "that which at first was not believed without scrutiny may afterwards be admitted without scruple." The neighbours' wondering inquiry, the witnesses agree, is the first link in a chain of testimony the rulers will labour in vain to break.

Henry frames the street-debate as a public weighing of evidence, two things being "debated in this conference" — whether this was indeed the man, and how the cure was wrought. Lightfoot recalls the very cadence of a Jerusalem beggar's plea, and Clarke notes that "beggars in all countries have a language peculiar to themselves." The witnesses agree that the man's notoriety is itself an apologetic asset: a stranger might be doubted, but a fixture of the temple gate, known to all, cannot be a planted fraud. The neighbours' wondering recognition, far from a mere narrative detail, is the first independent attestation that the once-blind beggar now sees.
9:
The divided murmur of the crowd — "Some said, This is he: others said, He is like him: but he said, I am he" — is read by the witnesses as both a token of the miracle's reality and a study in how a true narrative carries its own marks. The grammatical-historical Ellicott prefers the sharper reading, "No; but he is like him," by which the doubters "oppose... their own opinion, that it is a case of resemblance only," until "he himself sets the question at rest by declaring that he is the same person." Robertson hears in the Greek a "vigorous denial" and "mere similarity suggested," the crowd "divided," and notes the emphatic demonstrative — "that one spake up" — recurring through the chapter (vv.11, 25, 36).

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the evidential weight: the miracle "was not wrought in private — nor before a few persons — nor was it lightly credited." Those who knew the man "were divided in their opinion," not over whether the beggar had been blind — "this was known to all" — nor whether he now saw — "this was now notorious" — but only whether this seeing man was the very person they had known. The dispute itself, far from undermining the cure, establishes its outward facts beyond question.

The man's own three words — "I am he" — settle what the crowd cannot. Ellicott calls the verse "one of the many striking instances of the natural form" taken by a narrative told by an eyewitness; the witnesses find in the beggar's plain self-identification the same artless honesty that will carry him, undaunted, through every later interrogation.

Ellicott prizes the verse as "one of the many striking instances of the natural form" a true narrative takes when told by an eyewitness — the divided crowd, the confident doubters, the man's own quiet correction. Clarke insists the division was never over the substance: "whether the man who sat there begging was blind before — this was known to all; nor, whether the person now before them saw clearly — for this was now notorious," but only over his identity, which the man himself settles. Robertson's "that one spake up" catches the moment the object of the miracle steps forward to claim it; the wonder is so great that the crowd can scarcely believe the seeing man is the beggar they knew.
10:
The crowd's follow-up — "How were thine eyes opened?" — is brief, and the witnesses, almost wholly grammatical-historical, treat it as the natural next step once identity is settled. Robertson calls it the "natural and logical" question: "These neighbours admit the fact and want the manner ('how') of the cure made clear." Ellicott adds that the question gains force on the better reading of v.8 — that they had known him as a beggar — for it shows that "they knew he had been blind, the moment they knew that he was the same person who used to sit and beg."

What the witnesses notice is the pattern the question begins: the fact of the cure is conceded, and only the manner is pressed. This same move — granting the deed but probing endlessly into the how — will be repeated by the Pharisees (v.15, 26), and the beggar's patient retelling will become the very thread by which the truth is drawn out against his examiners. Here, among the neighbours, the inquiry is honest wonder; later, on the same words, it will harden into hostile cross-examination.

The simplicity of the exchange underscores what the chapter keeps pressing: the work of God stands as plain fact, and human questioning, whether wondering or malicious, can neither add to it nor explain it away. The neighbours ask how because the that is beyond dispute.

The question reveals the crowd's instinct to probe the method once the fact is beyond dispute — the same reflex that the Pharisees will exploit when honest wonder gives way to hostile cross-examination (v.15). Ellicott ties the inquiry to the better reading of v.8: knowing him as the blind beggar, they could only ask how. Robertson notes the form of the verb, a triple-augmented passive, and the plain logic — "these neighbours admit the fact and want the manner." The witnesses find here the pattern the whole chapter will press: the work of God stands as settled fact, and human questioning, whether kind or cruel, can neither explain it away nor improve upon the man's simple testimony that he now sees.
11:
The beggar's first account — "A man that is called Jesus made clay, and anointed mine eyes... and I received sight" — is read by the witnesses as a model of artless, exact testimony from one who does not yet know his Healer. The grammatical-historical Robertson stresses the man's limited knowledge: he speaks of "the man that is called Jesus," not yet knowing Him "as the Messiah the Son of God" (v.36); and the verb "received sight" means literally "to see again," not strictly true of one who "had never seen," so that "he got back sight that he had never had." Clarke admires the "simple and artless" narrative: the man "had never seen Jesus," so he does not say Jesus made clay of spittle — which he could not have watched — "but simply, he made clay, and spread it upon my eyes." Ellicott notes some manuscripts read "The Man that is called Jesus," implying the beggar and his friends had heard of His teaching.

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson of obedience rewarded: "So happy a result of obedience warns us to surmount every obstacle, and to proceed courageously wherever the Lord calls us," not doubting that "every thing which we undertake by his authority... will have a prosperous issue."

The Wesleyan Wesley fixes the beggar's starting-point precisely — "He seems to have been before totally ignorant of him" — which the witnesses make the measure of the spiritual progress to come. The man begins with a bare name and a plain fact; from this small seed, through every cross-examination, his knowledge and confession of Christ will steadily grow.

Ellicott observes that the man "gives the details in order, omitting the spitting on the ground, which he had not seen," and that the Greek for received sight means exactly "to see again" — a word stretched, in his unique case, to cover sight he had never possessed. Calvin turns the man's prompt obedience into a charge to every believer: the "happy result" warns us "to surmount every obstacle, and to proceed courageously wherever the Lord calls." The witnesses mark how exactly the man's report tracks his knowledge — a name, a touch he felt but could not watch, a command obeyed — the honest floor on which his rising confession will be built, from "a man called Jesus" here to the worshipped Son of Man at the chapter's end.
12:
The exchange "Where is he? He said, I know not" is brief, and the witnesses read it as marking both Christ's withdrawal and the rulers' designs. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the question is "the very question of" the hostile crowd at the feast (John 7:11), "Where is he?" Ellicott explains the man's honest ignorance: Jesus had "not returned" after sending him to the pool (v.7), and by the time the man had gone and come back, the Lord "would have gone beyond his knowledge."

The Reformed and Wesleyan commentators detect the menace behind the question. Gill judges it "put, not out of good will to Christ, but that they might apprehend him, and bring him before the sanhedrim, for doing work on the sabbath day," and Clarke agrees they "had designed to seize and deliver him up to the Sanhedrin, as a violater of the law." Gill weighs why Christ had withdrawn — whether to avoid the Jews' anticipated rage, or "to avoid all popular applause and glory, which he sought not" — and concludes it may have been "on both accounts."

The beggar's plain "I know not" is, for the witnesses, of a piece with his whole bearing: he reports only what he knows and claims no more. He cannot point them to Jesus, and would not if he could deliver Him to their malice; his ignorance, like his sight, is simply the truth. The withdrawal of Christ, meanwhile, leaves the healed man to bear witness alone before the council, which is exactly where the narrative now turns.

The witnesses weigh both the man's honesty and the questioners' design. Gill suspects the inquiry was "not out of good will to Christ" but to "apprehend him" for the sabbath, while granting Christ may have withdrawn both to escape their rage and to shun "popular applause and glory, which he sought not." Robertson hears the same words the hostile crowd used at the feast (John 7:11), and Ellicott explains the man's true ignorance — Jesus, having sent him to the pool, "would have gone beyond his knowledge" by the time he returned. His plain "I know not" is of a piece with his whole bearing: he claims only what he knows, and the withdrawn Christ leaves him to bear sole witness before the council.
13:
That "they brought to the Pharisees him that aforetime was blind" is read by the witnesses as the turn from wonder to prosecution. The grammatical-historical Lightfoot identifies the body: "The Pharisees, in this evangelist, are generally to be understood the Sanhedrim," John nowhere even mentioning the Sadducees. Robertson calls them "the accepted professional teachers who posed as knowing everything," and renders the vivid present, "they bring him." Barnes and Ellicott discern the motive: they did this "to accuse Jesus of having violated the Sabbath," in "the spirit of opposition," rather than "to examine into the evidence that he was from God." Clarke notes that with the Pharisees "every case of religion" found its final judgment, so the neighbours brought the man to learn whether the cure "had been done legally."

The Puritan Henry marks the irony that frames the whole episode: "One would have expected that such a miracle... would have settled his reputation," yet "instead of being embraced as a prophet... he is prosecuted as a criminal." The case meant to vindicate Christ becomes the occasion of His arraignment.

The Reformed Calvin draws the sober doctrine the narrative will illustrate: "wicked men are so far from profiting by the works of God" that the more they are "urged by their power," the more they "pour out the venom which dwells within." The restoration of sight "ought undoubtedly to have softened even hearts of stone," or at least to have held the Pharisees "in doubt" a while; that it instead hardens them is, for Calvin, the measure of a malice that no evidence can melt.

Lightfoot's identification of "the Pharisees" with the Sanhedrin — John nowhere even naming the Sadducees — frames the proceeding as an official tribunal, and Clarke notes that with this body "every case of religion" found its "final" judgment. Henry sharpens the irony that the miracle which should have "silenced and shamed all opposition" instead leads to the man's arraignment "as a criminal." Calvin reads the whole episode as proof that "wicked men are so far from profiting by the works of God" that they are "constrained to pour out the venom" within; a cure that "ought... to have softened even hearts of stone" only hardens them. The case opened to vindicate Christ becomes the occasion of His indictment.
14:
The note that "it was the sabbath day when Jesus made the clay, and opened his eyes" tells the witnesses why the cure became a trial, and they agree that for the rulers the day eclipsed the deed. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes the anarthrous "it was a sabbath," and that "to the Pharisees this fact was a far more important matter than whether or how the thing was done," given "the minute Sabbath regulations of the rabbis." Clarke and Wesley specify the breach: some rabbis held saliva a remedy for the eyes but reckoned its use "contrary to the law" on the sabbath, and the anointing of eyes with any medicine was "particularly forbidden by the tradition of the elders." Ellicott adds the further charge that "making the clay" was itself "a servile work which contravened the Sabbath," and (taking the day as the great day of the feast) sees the narrative following "in unbroken order."

The Reformed Calvin presses the deliberateness already seen in the earlier sabbath healing (John 5): Christ "purposely selected" the sabbath, which He "could easily have" avoided, "because the defense malignantly undertaken by men would tend to magnify the power of God." The offense, far from an accident, "serves as a whetstone" to sharpen their inquiry "more eagerly into the whole matter."

So the witnesses converge: the sabbath frame is the hinge on which the rulers' hostility turns, and it is, on Calvin's reading, Christ's own choosing. The Pharisees will fasten on the day and pass over the wonder — and in fastening on it, they will only spread the testimony they meant to suppress.

Robertson directs the reader to the wider discussion of "the minute Sabbath regulations of the rabbis," for which the bare fact "it was a sabbath" outweighed every question of whether or how the cure was done. Ellicott takes the day as the great feast-day (John 7:37) and the narrative as running "in unbroken order," the making of clay reckoned a "servile work." Calvin's point governs: Christ "purposely selected" the sabbath, as He had with the paralytic (John 5), foreseeing that the rulers' malicious defense would only "magnify the power of God," the offense serving as a "whetstone" to drive their inquiry deeper. The day they seize upon becomes the means by which the testimony spreads.
15:
When "the Pharisees also asked him how he had received his sight," the witnesses notice both the narrowing of the question and the beggar's weary brevity. The grammatical-historical Barnes makes the methodological point: "the proper question... was whether he had in fact done it, and not in what way," just as the real question about a sinner's conversion is whether it has happened, "not about the mode or manner." Ellicott observes the man's answer is "the same as before, but briefer" — the reply "of a man who is answering against his will" (cf. v.27) — and that his new phrase "and do see" speaks "as in conscious possession of the power to see." Robertson marks the inchoative imperfect, the Pharisees "began also to question him," with "no denial as yet of the fact, only interest in the 'how.'"

The Reformed Calvin draws the legal manoeuvre: the Pharisees, now made "witnesses" of the confession the people had already heard, "leaving out of view the question as to the fact," chose to "dispute only about the law of the case" — not denying the cure, but finding "a crime in the circumstance" of the sabbath. Their whole strategy is to concede the deed and condemn the day.

Barnes presses his analogy: as the Pharisees ask how rather than whether, so "no small part of the disputes and inquiries among men are about the mode in which the Spirit renews the heart, and not about the evidence that it is done." Ellicott notes the man's terse "and do see" speaks "as in conscious possession of the power," answering, like one weary, only as much as he must (cf. v.27). Robertson marks the inchoative imperfect — they "began also to question him" — with no denial yet of the fact. Calvin exposes the legal strategy: the Pharisees, made "witnesses" of the confession, "leaving out of view the question as to the fact," chose to "dispute only about the law," finding "a crime in the circumstance" of the sabbath. The deed conceded, the day condemned — that is their whole case.
16:
The verse records a genuine division within the council — "This man is not of God, because he keepeth not the sabbath day" against "How can a man that is a sinner do such miracles?" — and the witnesses read it as truth splitting the very tribunal. The grammatical-historical Robertson lays out the first party's reasoning: he "violates our rules about the Sabbath and therefore is a Sabbath-breaker" (John 5:10, 16), hence "not from God"; while the second question repeats "the argument of Nicodemus" (John 3:2). Barnes exposes the flaw: "they assumed that their views of the Sabbath were correct," never asking whether "the interpretation which they put on the law might not be erroneous." Clarke calls it false reasoning, since "the Sabbath was made for man, and not man for the Sabbath," and works of mercy were never forbidden.

The Reformed Calvin defines the charged word: "sinner" here denotes "a person of immoral conduct and a despiser of God" (as in Mark 2:16), the enemies inferring from the sabbath-breach that Christ was "profane... and destitute of all religion." Wesley likewise glosses the better party's question — how can "one living in wilful sin" do such signs? The schism, the witnesses agree, is itself a triumph of the evidence: the miracle is so plain that it divides even those assembled to condemn its Author.

Robertson supplies the causal force — "because he keepeth not the sabbath" is reason enough for the first party to brand Him a sabbath-breaker (John 5:16) and so "not from God." Clarke answers their premise: the sabbath law "never forbade any work but what was of the servile and unnecessary kind," and "works of necessity and mercy never could be forbidden" by the God "whose name is mercy." Ellicott shows the inference each side draws — the one, that a sabbath-breaker's power "cannot be of God" and so must be "of evil"; the other, the "better party," that "a man that is a sinner" could not do such signs. Calvin warns against the neutral who will not judge between them: the very schism, the witnesses agree, is the miracle forcing the council against itself.
17:
Pressed again, the beggar answers "He is a prophet," and the witnesses mark both the Pharisees' shifting tactic and the man's advancing confession. The Antiochene Chrysostom notes the telling change of question: having said "This man is not of God, because he keepeth not the Sabbath," they now ask not about the sabbath but "What sayest thou of him, that he hath opened thine eyes?" — quietly dropping the ground they could not hold. The grammatical-historical Barnes paraphrases the man's reply as reasoning from the deed: the power "to work such a miracle proves that he is sent from God," and Ellicott observes that the assembly generally, having "differed among themselves" (v.16), now seek "what impression the fact... had left upon him who was the object of it." Robertson: "the doctors disagree and they ask the patient."

The Reformed Calvin turns the scene into a parable of truth under pressure: "The more diligently they inquire, the more impressively does the truth of God appear; for they act as if one were endeavoring to extinguish a strong flame by his breath." Wherever the wicked "contrive all that they can to crush the truth of God," we need not fear, for all they gain is "to cause its light to burn with greater brightness." The man's confession — already past "the man called Jesus" to "a prophet" — is the flame their breath only fans.

Chrysostom presses the duty to read "with all exactness," noting the tell-tale shift: they no longer ask about the broken sabbath but "What sayest thou of him, that he hath opened thine eyes?" — abandoning the ground they cannot hold. Ellicott observes the question comes from "the assembly generally," seeking "what impression the fact... had left upon" the very man it concerned. Clarke discerns a trap: they hoped, by getting him to "acknowledge Christ for the Messiah," to "put him out of the synagogue" (v.22), but "the wisdom of God taught him how to escape the snares." Calvin's image holds the scene together: the harder they puff, the higher the flame, for the man's confession only climbs — from a name, to "a prophet."
18:
That "the Jews did not believe... that he had been blind, and received his sight, until they had called the parents" shows the witnesses a hostility now driven to deny the very fact. The grammatical-historical Robertson explains the unbelief: the man's testimony "conflicted with their theological views of God and the Sabbath," so "they refused belief" and summoned the parents. Ellicott marks the loaded designation — not "the Pharisees" as in v.16, but "the Jews," the term of settled opposition — and that "because of this explanation of the fact, they are driven to the expedient of disbelieving the fact itself." Barnes catalogues the "three questions" by which they hoped to "intimidate the parents" into a self-contradiction.

The Latin and Antiochene Catena finds the moral in their failure: "it is the nature of truth, to be strengthened by the very snares that are laid against it," whereas "a lie is" betrayed by them. The Reformed Calvin presses that their blindness "was voluntary": nothing prevented them "from seeing an obvious work of God" but their own "perverse hatred of Christ." The Puritan Henry draws the proverb that explains the parents' coming caution — "the fear of man brings a snare" (Proverbs 29:25) — which "often makes people deny and disown Christ."

The Catena's fathers make the rulers' disbelief a backhanded tribute: "it is the nature of truth, to be strengthened by the very snares that are laid against it," whereas a lie unravels under scrutiny. Barnes lists the three questions designed to "intimidate the parents" into a self-defeating answer, and Robertson notes the construction — they withheld belief "until they called the parents." Ellicott marks the ominous shift in designation from "the Pharisees" to "the Jews," the term of fixed enmity, "driven to the expedient of disbelieving the fact itself." Calvin names the cause as "voluntary" blindness, a "perverse hatred of Christ," and Henry the proverb that explains the coming timidity — "the fear of man brings a snare" (Proverbs 29:25).

Augustine's gloss in the Catena fixes the bare fact the rulers fought — the man "had been blind, and now saw" — and it is exactly this undeniable change that drives them, having failed to deny the cure, to the desperate course of denying the man.
19:
The parents are met with a triple question — "Is this your son, who ye say was born blind? how then doth he now see?" — and the witnesses see in it a calculated trap. The grammatical-historical Ellicott unfolds the three: "Is this your son? Do ye still say that he was born blind?" — which, given that "he now possesses the faculty of sight" (v.32), they paint as incredible — "and if you do, how do you account for the fact that he now sees?" The "ye" is emphatic: "ye say he was born blind, as opposed to us, for we do not believe it." Robertson agrees it was "shrewdly put with three questions in one in order to confuse the parents... and give the hostile Pharisees a handle."

The Reformed Calvin reads the same craft and its overruling: having failed by one route, "they now attempt another," but "the Lord not only defeats their attempts... but turns them even to an opposite purpose," for out of "a variety of entangled and captious questions" the parents "select only the half" they can safely answer. The trap meant to convict the beggar of fraud instead extracts a confession that he was indeed born blind and now sees.

Gill notes the sharp purpose of the first question: had the parents disowned him, the rulers "would have got an advantage... and would have convicted him of a lie." Ellicott unfolds the whole as three barbs in one — your son? still born blind, which is "incredible" now he sees (v.32)? then how does he see? — the emphatic "ye say" setting the parents' word against the rulers' disbelief. Robertson agrees it was "shrewdly put... in order to confuse the parents" and hand the Pharisees "a handle." Calvin discerns the craft and its overruling: out of "a variety of entangled and captious questions" the parents "select only the half" they can safely answer, and the trap meant to convict the beggar of fraud instead extracts sworn confirmation that he was born blind and now sees.

Gill adds that proving such a lie upon the beggar would at once have discredited his whole testimony and the miracle with it; the parents' truthful answer therefore not only escaped the trap but secured the very fact the council most wished to bury.
20:
The parents' careful reply — "We know that this is our son, and that he was born blind" — is read by the witnesses as conceding everything that mattered while withholding what they feared to say. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that these "two questions the parents answer clearly and thus cut the ground from under the disbelief of these Pharisees as to the fact of the cure" (v.18) — "so these Pharisees made a failure here." Barnes explains the limit of their testimony: the first two questions they answer "without hesitation," but the third "they could not positively answer, as they had not witnessed the means of the cure," and "were afraid to express their belief"; though they "had themselves no doubt," they were "not eye-witnesses, and could not be therefore legal evidence." Ellicott: they know, "as none besides themselves can know," the painful truth that he was born blind.

The Reformed Calvin turns to their silence about the cure: "this latter point... they pass by, because it would give offense," and "by their silence they show their ingratitude." Having received "so distinguished a gift of God," they "ought to have burned with desire to celebrate his name"; instead, "struck with terror, they bury the grace of God" as far as they can. Their admission establishes the fact; their fear forfeits the praise.

Barnes explains why the parents stop where they do: they could affirm sonship and congenital blindness with certainty, but the cure itself they "had not witnessed," and being "not eye-witnesses" could give "no legal evidence," besides being "afraid." Robertson judges that by these two clear answers the parents "cut the ground from under the disbelief of these Pharisees," who "made a failure here." Calvin turns to what their fear costs: having received "so distinguished a gift of God," they "ought to have burned with desire to celebrate his name," but "struck with terror, they bury the grace of God" so far as they can. Their testimony settles the fact and forfeits the praise — the very ingratitude their bold son will not share.
21:
The parents' evasion — "but by what means he now seeth, we know not... he is of age; ask him" — is read by the witnesses as a careful retreat into bare fact. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes they "profess ignorance both as to the 'how'... and the 'who,'" not being "witnesses of the cure" but having "the story only from the son." Ellicott shows they answer "in the exact words of the question," refusing to "pass beyond the plain matters of fact of which they were certain," and that they perceive a fourth, unspoken question — "who hath opened his eyes" — "the real point... in which they have determined not to be entangled." Clarke explains "he is of age": literally "he has stature," a full-grown man, mature age being reckoned among the Jews at thirty.

The Reformed Calvin had already named the fear that drives the evasion (v.20): dread of excommunication makes the parents push the witness onto their son. Their "ask him" is at once true — he is the competent witness — and cowardly, a way of escaping the danger themselves. The narrative thus throws the whole testimony back upon the healed man, who alone will prove unafraid, and whose bold confession will stand in sharp relief against his parents' caution.

Robertson notes the parents "profess ignorance both as to the 'how'... and the 'who,'" retreating to what they had only "from the son." Ellicott shows they answer "in the exact words of the question," refusing to "pass beyond the plain matters of fact," and detect a fourth, unspoken question — who opened his eyes — "in which they have determined not to be entangled." Clarke explains the legal-sounding "he is of age": literally "he has stature," mature age being reckoned among the Jews at thirty. Calvin names the spring of it all — dread of the synagogue-ban (v.22) — which makes "ask him" at once truthful and cowardly, throwing the whole weight of witness onto the one man in the room who will not flinch.

The witnesses note the parents' formula throws the decisive testimony onto the one competent and fearless witness; their caution, however prudent, surrenders to their son the honour — and the danger — of confessing how his eyes were opened.
22:
The Evangelist's explanation — "because they feared the Jews: for the Jews had agreed already, that if any man did confess that he was Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue" — draws the witnesses to the practice of excommunication and the fear it bred. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the continuing fear (imperfect, "a continuing fear and not without reason") already seen at John 7:13, and the past-perfect "had agreed," a compact "to form" (the same word used of the covenant with Judas, Luke 22:5). Ellicott clarifies that this "does not imply a formal decree of the Sanhedrin, but an agreement on the part of the leaders." Lightfoot and Clarke describe the penalty: the lesser excommunication, nidui, a separation "from all religious connection," as distinct from the cherem or anathema, which "was not used against the followers of Christ till after the resurrection."

The Reformed Calvin uses the verse to vindicate proper church discipline while condemning its abuse: "the custom of excommunication is ancient, and has been observed in all ages," rightly used "against apostates and despisers of the Law," but here "turned against the disciples of Christ." The institution is from "the most ancient discipline of the Church"; its perversion is the rulers' crime. The Wesleyan Wesley simply glosses the threat — to be "excommunicated" — the very fear that silences the parents and will soon fall upon their son.

Lightfoot distinguishes the grades of the ban: whether the "ordinary" exclusion from a synagogue or the graver cutting-off "from the whole congregation of Israel" by the Sanhedrin's president. Clarke names the lesser form nidui, the fuller cherem or anathema coming against Christ's followers only "after the resurrection." Robertson stresses the past-perfect "had agreed," a settled compact — the same verb used of the bargain with Judas (Luke 22:5). Calvin uses the verse to honour proper discipline while condemning its abuse: excommunication is "ancient, and has been observed in all ages" against "apostates and despisers," but here is "turned against the disciples of Christ" — a sacred instrument bent to a wicked end, and the fear that now governs the parents.
23:
The brief repetition — "Therefore said his parents, He is of age; ask him" — the witnesses treat as the Evangelist underscoring the motive just disclosed. The grammatical-historical Robertson glosses the connective: "For this reason" — that is, "reason enough for due caution," the dread of the synagogue-ban hanging over every word. Ellicott simply refers back to his note on v.21, the parents holding to the safe ground of fact and pushing the dangerous testimony onto their son.

What the witnesses see repeated here is the contrast the chapter is building. The parents, who have most cause to glory in their son's healing, are governed by fear; the son, who has most to lose, will be governed by the truth. Their twice-stated "ask him" is the hinge: it removes them from danger and sets the healed man alone before the council, where the decisive confession — and the decisive expulsion — will come.

The repetition also serves the narrative's realism, the kind of true-to-life detail the grammatical-historical readers prize: a frightened answer, given once under pressure, is given again almost word for word. The parents have found a formula that keeps them safe, and they cling to it. Behind their caution stands the whole machinery of intimidation the rulers have built, and against it, in the next verses, the beggar's lone honesty will be thrown into the sharpest relief.

Robertson reads the repeated formula as "reason enough for due caution," the synagogue-ban hanging over every syllable, and Ellicott simply refers back to the parents' careful stand at v.21. The witnesses find in the bare restatement the chapter's sharpening contrast: the parents, with most cause to glory, are ruled by fear; their son, with most to lose, will be ruled by truth. Their twice-given "ask him" is the narrative hinge, removing them from danger and leaving the healed man alone before the tribunal. Behind their caution stands the whole apparatus of intimidation the rulers have built — and against it, in the verses to follow, the beggar's lone, fearless honesty is thrown into the boldest relief.

Gill simply refers the reader back to the parents' earlier words, the Evangelist's repetition underscoring that fear, not ignorance, governs their reply; they know more than they will say, and say only what keeps them clear of the synagogue's ban.
24:
Summoning the man a second time with "Give God the praise: we know that this man is a sinner," the rulers use a solemn formula, and the witnesses are largely agreed on its force. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson identify it as an adjuration — the very words by which Joshua put Achan on oath (Joshua 7:19; cf. 1 Samuel 6:5) — "equivalent to an adjuration in the presence of God to acknowledge the truth." Robertson stresses it "does not mean gratitude to God as in" the cleansed leper (Luke 17:18), but a charge to confess; and the demand was now "like a joke unless the Pharisees meant to imply that his previous story was untrue."

The Latin and Antiochene Catena reads the phrase as a mask. Chrysostom: they "conceal their object under the presence of religion," wanting the man to "confess that this man has had nothing to do with the work" — which Augustine calls not giving God glory but "rather to blaspheme Him." Alcuin notes they wished him "to give glory to God, by calling Christ a sinner, as they did."

The Reformed Calvin sees them "constrained by shame" to recall the man they had found "too firm," and observes the more fiercely they "struggle against God, the more numerous are the cords which they put about their neck." The Puritan Henry lifts the beggar's coming reply into a parable of grace: "the most powerful and lasting affections to Christ arise from actual knowledge of him," for though we cannot always tell "when, and how... the blessed change was wrought," we may take comfort if we can say, "Whereas I was blind, now I see."

Robertson notes the summons was really the third time the man faced them (cf. the "again" of v.17), and that "give glory to God" here is no thanksgiving (unlike Luke 17:18) but a sworn adjuration to tell the truth (Joshua 7:19). Alcuin sees them wanting him to "give glory to God" precisely "by calling Christ a sinner, as they did," and Chrysostom asks why, if so, they had not convicted Christ when He challenged, "Which of you convinces Me of sin?" Calvin marks how "the more fiercely they struggle against God, the more numerous are the cords which they put about their neck," while Henry hears, in the confession they are about to provoke, the abiding comfort of every redeemed soul: "Whereas I was blind, now I see."
25:
The beggar's celebrated answer — "Whether he be a sinner or no, I know not: one thing I know, that, whereas I was blind, now I see" — is read by the witnesses as the masterstroke of an honest man refusing a trap. The grammatical-historical Robertson admires his keenness: he "refuses to fall into the trap set for him," passing by their quibble about Jesus being a "sinner" and clinging "to the one fact of his own experience." Clarke notes he "pays no attention to their cavils," but "in the simplicity of his heart, speaks to the fact," ready to give "the most substantial evidence." Ellicott observes he will, "like his parents," confine himself to "matters of fact coming under his own certain knowledge," not disputing their assertion but setting his own beside it, "which cannot be" gainsaid.

The Reformed Calvin reads a sharper edge: the man "spoke ironically, in order to wound them more deeply." Having already confessed Christ "a prophet" (v.17) and seen that it gained nothing, "he suspends his judgment about the person, and brings forward the fact itself," so that the undeniable cure does the arguing. Whatever the tone — plain simplicity (Clarke, Ellicott) or pointed irony (Calvin) — the witnesses agree the man has found the one ground his examiners cannot take from him: his own opened eyes.

Robertson admires the man's economy: he "passes by their quibbling" about Jesus being a sinner and "clings to the one fact of his own experience." Clarke finds him speaking "in the simplicity of his heart" to a fact he can prove with "the most substantial evidence," and Ellicott that he, like his parents, will keep to "matters of fact coming under his own certain knowledge," not disputing their verdict but setting beside it his own, "which cannot be" denied. Calvin alone hears a deliberate sting — the man "spoke ironically, in order to wound them more deeply," having found that confessing Christ "a prophet" (v.17) gained nothing. Whether plain or pointed, his word is unanswerable: the cure is his, and no cross-examination can take it from him.
26:
The renewed question — "What did he to thee? how opened he thine eyes?" — is read by the witnesses as a transparent attempt to make the man stumble. The grammatical-historical Barnes states the aim plainly: they ask "so often" in order "to draw him into a contradiction; either to intimidate him, or throw him off his guard, so that he might be detected in denying what he had before affirmed." Yet, he adds, "God gave to this poor man grace and strength to make a bold confession of the truth, and sufficient common sense completely to confound his proud and subtle examiners." Robertson notes it is "another cross-examination," now even admitting the cure and again pressing only the "how" (cf. vv.15, 17). Ellicott suggests the repeated questions may be merely "to gain time or cover their retreat," his "honest boldness" being "too much for their craft."

The Reformed Calvin turns the contrast into a rebuke of believers' coldness: "When we see wicked men so delighted in performing their own base actions, we ought to be ashamed of our slothfulness, in acting with such coolness about the affairs of Christ." Though they "search on all sides to obtain grounds of slander," the Lord "defeats their attempts... by the unshaken firmness of the blind man," who not only holds his ground but "freely and severely reproaches them."

Barnes reads the repeated "how opened he thine eyes?" as a deliberate effort to make the man "deny what he had before affirmed," defeated because "God gave to this poor man grace and strength" to "confound his proud and subtle examiners." Robertson notes the questioners now even concede the cure, pressing only the "how" a third time (cf. vv.15, 17). Ellicott suspects the reiteration was "merely to gain time or cover their retreat," the man's "honest boldness" proving "too much for their craft." Calvin turns the contrast on the reader: if the wicked are so tireless "in performing their own base actions," believers ought to "be ashamed" of their coolness in Christ's cause — for the Lord defeats every snare "by the unshaken firmness of the blind man."
27:
The beggar's stinging retort — "I have told you already, and ye did not hear: wherefore would ye hear it again? will ye also be his disciples?" — the witnesses read as the turning of the tables, sharp with irony. The grammatical-historical Robertson catches the keenness: the negative question "will ye also become his disciples?" expects a no, but carries "the keenest irony in this gibe," the "also" betraying that the man knows Jesus already has disciples and that the Pharisees know it too (cf. John 7:45–52). Clarke reads him weary and shrewd: "Why then do you wish to hear it again? Is it because ye wish to become his disciples?" — knowing "he should soon put an end to the debate." Ellicott notes the man "becomes weary of this cross-questioning," and will not even repeat his earlier, briefest answer.

The Wesleyan Wesley hears a gentler note beneath the irony — "As well as I, at length convinced and willing to be his disciples?" — as if the man half-wished it true. Either way, the witnesses agree the once-passive beggar has seized the offensive: the man dragged in to be examined now examines his judges, and the question he flings at the Sanhedrin — whether they too will follow Jesus — is the most cutting thing said in the whole proceeding.

Robertson finds in the man's gibe "the keenest irony": the little word "also" betrays that he knows Jesus already has disciples, and that the Pharisees know it too (cf. John 7:45–52). Clarke pictures him weary of repetition — "Why then do you wish to hear it again?" — and certain that the question "will ye also be his disciples?" will "put an end to the debate." Ellicott notes he will not even repeat his earlier, briefest answer. Wesley alone catches a wistful undertone, as if the man half-wished it true — "at length convinced and willing to be his disciples." The witnesses agree the reversal is complete: the man hauled in to be examined now examines his judges, and his question is the sharpest blade drawn in the whole hearing.
28:
The council's furious reply — "Thou art his disciple; but we are Moses' disciples" — together with the reviling that prompts it, shows the witnesses malice exposed as argument fails. The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott note the rare verb for reviled (elsewhere only at Acts 23:4; 1 Peter 2:23), "the passionate outburst of their anger," and the scornful "of that fellow" they fling with "his disciple." Barnes lays bare the false antithesis: they "held Jesus to be a violater and contemner of the law of Moses," and so cast discipleship to Him as treason against Moses. Lightfoot supplies the contemporary phrase — to be "Moses' disciples" was a recognised boast — and Clarke, parsing the etymology of revile, finds them speaking "cutting, piercing words" like "the piercings of a sword" (Proverbs 12:18).

The Reformed Calvin probes the "plausible pretence" beneath the boast: "they are afraid of revolting" from the Law, holding that one "could not be a disciple of Christ without revolting from the Law of Moses," and so "expressly represent these two things as inconsistent." The tragedy, the witnesses agree, is that the very Moses they claim wrote of the Christ they revile — a contradiction the next verses will expose, even as the rulers wield Moses' name as a weapon against the man who has confessed the truth.

Robertson and Ellicott note the rare word for reviled — found elsewhere only at Acts 23:4 and 1 Peter 2:23 — marking "the passionate outburst of their anger," and the scornful "of that fellow" hurled with "his disciple." Clarke, parsing the etymology, hears them speak "cutting, piercing words" like a sword (Proverbs 12:18). Barnes exposes the false antithesis on which their boast rests — they held Christ "a violater and contemner of the law of Moses," and so discipleship to Him as treason against Moses. Calvin probes the "plausible pretence" beneath it: a fear of "revolting" from the Law that makes Moses and Christ enemies — though the next verses will turn that very Moses into a witness against them.

The witnesses observe the deepest irony of the boast: the Moses whose disciples they claim to be had himself written of the Christ they revile (John 5:46), so that their loyalty to the Law, rightly understood, would have led them straight to the One they cast out.
29:
The council's boast — "We know that God spake unto Moses: as for this fellow, we know not from whence he is" — the witnesses read as a feigned ignorance covering settled hostility. The grammatical-historical commentators clarify what "whence" means. Calvin and Wesley: it refers not to birthplace but to "the prophetical office" — "by what power and authority he does these things" — the rulers alleging "they have no knowledge of his calling, so as to receive him as having proceeded from God." Robertson marks the perfect "hath spoken" (so still "on record," Exodus 33:11; Hebrews 1:1) and the contemptuous "this fellow" in emphatic position; he notes the irony that "some of the people did" claim to know whence Jesus was (John 7:27). Barnes grants they were correct that God commissioned Moses, "but they assumed their interpretation of the law to be infallible, and hence condemned Jesus."

Clarke states their reasoning bluntly: "We have the fullest assurance that the commission of Moses was Divine; but we have no proof that this man has such a commission." The witnesses see the rulers retreating, from the failed denial of the fact, to a denial of Christ's authority — opposing the certainty of Moses to a Teacher whose credentials they pretend not to recognise. It is precisely this manufactured ignorance — "ye know not from whence he is" — that the healed man will seize upon and turn against them in the next verse.

Calvin and Wesley insist the "whence" is not geography but "the prophetical office" — by what "power and authority" Jesus acts — the rulers pleading ignorance of His calling so as not to receive Him. Robertson notes the perfect "hath spoken," Moses' commission standing "on record" (Exodus 33:11; Hebrews 1:1), the contemptuous "this fellow" set in emphatic contrast, and the irony that some had claimed precisely to know "whence" Jesus was (John 7:27). Barnes grants the truth of their first clause while exposing the flaw of the second — they "assumed their interpretation of the law to be infallible." Clarke states their retreat baldly: certain of Moses, they pretend to "no proof" of this Teacher — and on that manufactured ignorance the beggar will pounce.
30:
The beggar's astonished reply — "Why herein is a marvellous thing, that ye know not from whence he is, and yet he hath opened mine eyes" — the witnesses celebrate as the layman confounding the learned. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the man is now "angry... and quick in his insight," seizing the very contradiction: "You confess your ignorance of whence he is, ye who know everything, and yet he opened my eyes." Barnes and Clarke draw out the irony — "Is it possible that such persons as you... whose business it is to distinguish good from evil... cannot decide in a case so plain?" Could any but one "endued with the power of God" open the eyes of a man born blind?

The Reformed Calvin reads the man "indirectly reprov[ing] them" for remaining "unmoved by a miracle so illustrious," and pretending not to know Christ's calling, "as if... such a testimony of Divine power should be held in no estimation." To "show more clearly their stupidity or malice," he magnifies the wonder of the deed.

The Wesleyan Wesley marvels at the instrument: "Utterly illiterate as he was. And with what strength and clearness of reason!" — for "so had God opened the eyes of his understanding, as well as his bodily eyes." That "the teachers and guides of the people" should fail to see what a healed beggar sees so plainly — that a worker of an unheard-of miracle "must be from heaven, sent by God" — is, for all the witnesses, the chapter's running irony made explicit: spiritual sight has passed from the rulers to the man they despise.

Robertson finds the man now "angry... and quick in his insight," seizing the contradiction with a sting: "You confess your ignorance of whence he is, ye who know everything, and yet he opened my eyes." Barnes and Clarke draw the irony toward the council's office — "such persons as you... whose business it is to distinguish good from evil" cannot decide "in a case so plain"? Calvin sees him "indirectly reprov[ing] them" for treating "such a testimony of Divine power" as nothing. Wesley marvels at the instrument — "utterly illiterate... and with what strength and clearness of reason!" — for God had opened "the eyes of his understanding, as well as his bodily eyes." The running irony of the chapter here becomes explicit: spiritual sight has passed from the rulers to the beggar they despise.
31:
The man's argument — "Now we know that God heareth not sinners: but if any man be a worshipper of God, and doeth his will, him he heareth" — the witnesses read as a syllogism turned on the Pharisees from their own premises. The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets it out: "(1) God heareth not sinners, but only those who worship Him... (2) That God heareth this Man is certain, for such a miracle could be performed only by divine power. (3) This Man, therefore, is not a sinner, but is from God." Robertson notes this was the very argument of the better Pharisees at v.16, and is "frequent in the O.T." (Job 27:9; Psalm 66:18; Isaiah 1:15), the conclusion "inevitable from this premise."

The witnesses are careful to bound the maxim. The Reformed Gill and Calvin, with Wesley, read "sinners" not as all who sin — for "God hears" every penitent — but as "notorious" or "impenitent" sinners, "an ungodly and immoral person"; it is Scripture's "uniform doctrine, that God does not listen to any but those who call upon him with truth and sincerity." The Wesleyan Clarke suggests the word may even mean "heathens, or persons not proselyted," set in contrast to the "worshipper of God." Wesley honours the speaker: "the honest courage of this man in adhering to the truth, though he knew the consequence" (v.22), "gives him claim to the title of a confessor."

Ellicott lays the man's reasoning out as a formal syllogism, its major premise — "God heareth not sinners" — granted on every hand and grounded in the Old Testament (Job 27:9; Isaiah 1:15). The witnesses bound the maxim carefully: Gill, Calvin, and Wesley read "sinners" not as all who sin — for God hears the penitent — but as the "notorious" and "impenitent," while Clarke suggests it may mean "heathens... not proselyted," set against the true "worshipper of God." Wesley crowns the man with a title for his courage "though he knew the consequence" (v.22) — "a confessor." The untaught beggar has out-argued the Sanhedrin from premises the Sanhedrin itself supplied.
32:
The man clinches his case — "Since the world began was it not heard that any man opened the eyes of one that was born blind" — and the witnesses note that the stress falls on the unprecedented, congenital nature of the cure. The grammatical-historical Barnes: "Neither Moses nor any of the prophets had ever done this... no instance of this kind is recorded in the Old Testament," so the man "argued justly" that its Author "must be from God"; and since Jesus worked "not by surgical operations, but by clay," it showed He could heal "by any means." Robertson stresses the perfect participle, "of a man born blind" — "this is the chief point and the man will not let it be overlooked, almost rubs it in." Ellicott hears in "since the world began" a "reminiscence of" Isaiah 64:4, and notes that "no science or skill had at that time been equal to the removal" of congenital blindness.

Clarke frames the inference as a challenge to the council: "if this person then were not the best of beings, would God grant him a privilege which he has hitherto denied to his choicest favorites?" The miracle is not merely great but unique — outside the whole record of Moses and the prophets — and on that uniqueness the man rests his whole conclusion. The very point the Pharisees most wish to bury, the witnesses observe, is the one he drives home: the eyes that never saw now see.

Barnes presses the point that "neither Moses nor any of the prophets had ever done this," so the man "argued justly" from the unheard-of deed to its divine Author, and that Christ's use of clay rather than "surgical operations" showed He could heal "by any means." Robertson notes how the man "will not let it be overlooked, almost rubs it in" — a man born blind. Ellicott hears "since the world began" echo Isaiah 64:4, and observes that "no science or skill" of that age could touch congenital blindness. Clarke frames the challenge to the council: would God grant "a privilege which he has hitherto denied to his choicest favorites" to one who was not "the best of beings"? The uniqueness of the cure carries the man's whole argument.
33:
The conclusion — "If this man were not of God, he could do nothing" — the witnesses receive as the unanswerable point that provokes the man's expulsion. The grammatical-historical Robertson identifies the grammar precisely: a "negative condition of second class" with imperfect indicative, assuming Jesus is not "from God" (the rulers' charge at v.16) only to conclude "he could do nothing" — and so "the man has scored" against them. Barnes calls the reasoning "conclusive": "the fact that Jesus could perform miracles like this was full proof... One such miracle proves that he was from God." Ellicott shows how the argument "meets each of their assertions" — the general maxim of v.31 denied their charge that He was a sinner, and this conclusion denies their charge that He was "not of God."

Clarke states the theology behind it: "God is the fountain of all good: all good must proceed from him," so one who works "such beneficent miracles" must be "commissioned by the good God." Wesley adds the limiting phrase — He could do "nothing of this kind; nothing miraculous." The witnesses agree that the once-blind beggar, untaught and lately a object of charity, has out-reasoned the Sanhedrin on its own ground; and it is this very victory of plain truth, not any fault, that will get him driven from the synagogue.

Robertson identifies the precise grammar — a "negative condition of second class" with imperfect indicative — granting the rulers' premise (v.16) only to crush it: if Jesus were not from God "he could do nothing," so "the man has scored." Barnes calls the reasoning "conclusive," a proof that "never has been and never can be refuted": one such miracle settles the question. Ellicott shows the argument answering the council point for point — the maxim of v.31 denied that Christ was a sinner, and this conclusion denies that He was "not of God." Clarke grounds it in first principles: "God is the fountain of all good," and no good can flow but through Him. The beggar's logic, the witnesses agree, is precisely what gets him cast out.
34:
The council's final fury — "Thou wast altogether born in sins, and dost thou teach us? And they cast him out" — the witnesses read as malice substituting for argument, and as the making of the first Christian confessor. The grammatical-historical Robertson parses the contemptuous "in sins thou wast begotten all of thee," the word "altogether" a predicate that "teaches total depravity in this case beyond controversy, the Pharisees being judges," and the outburst "and dost thou teach us?" as "the audacity of it all" — "insufferable," since he had "utterly routed them." Barnes states the principle: "When men have no arguments, they attempt to supply their place by revilings," reproaching their adversary "with crime." Ellicott hears them cast "the calamity of his birth" in his teeth as "the mark of special sin" — reverting to the very view Christ rejected at v.3.

The Antiochene Chrysostom turns the expulsion into honour: "they who for the sake of the truth and the confession of Christ suffer anything terrible... are especially honored," as fell out with this man. Lightfoot makes him "the first confessor in the Christian church, as John the Baptist had been the first martyr," suffering excommunication "for the name of Christ." The Reformed Calvin notes they "alluded... to his blindness," the scribes being convinced that souls bore in new bodies "the punishment of their former crimes." Their casting-out, the witnesses agree, is at once the rulers' defeat and the man's crowning.

Robertson hears in "and dost thou teach us?" the council's outrage at "the audacity of it all" — a routed tribunal lashing out — and reads "altogether born in sins" as teaching, in this taunt, "total depravity... the Pharisees being judges." Barnes states the rule: "When men have no arguments, they attempt to supply their place by revilings." Calvin notes they fling his blindness in his face, the scribes holding that souls bore in new bodies "the punishment of their former crimes" — the very error Christ rejected at v.3. Chrysostom turns the disgrace to glory — those who "suffer anything terrible" for confessing Christ are "especially honored" — and Lightfoot makes the man "the first confessor in the Christian church," cast out for the Name.
35:
That "Jesus heard that they had cast him out; and when he had found him, he said, Dost thou believe on the Son of God?" turns the witnesses to a textual question and a tender providence. On the text, the grammatical-historical Robertson judges that, against the reading "Son of God" (A L Θ and most versions), the older manuscripts (Aleph B D W, Old Syriac) read "Son of Man," "almost certainly correct"; either way it is "a distinct Messianic claim quite beyond the range of this man's limited knowledge." Clarke notes that "Dost thou believe on the Son of God?" is the same as asking belief in "the Messiah," the titles being inseparable (John 1:49; Matthew 16:16). Barnes marks the man's readiness: he "believed according to the light he had," and now showed himself "prepared to believe all that Jesus said" — "the nature of true faith."

The Antiochene and Latin Catena dwells on the seeking Shepherd: "the Jews cast him out of the temple, and the Lord of the temple found him," receiving him "as the judge doth the wrestler after his labors, and crowned him." The Puritan Henry presses the same tenderness — Christ "sought him, and looked after him, that he might encourage and comfort him," rewarding the man who had "boldly" defended Him. The Reformed Calvin draws the consolation: the "excommunications of the enemies of Christ" are "trivial" and not to be dreaded, for the one cast out by them is sought and owned by the Son of God Himself.

The Catena's fathers frame the rescue as a coronation: "the Jews cast him out of the temple, and the Lord of the temple found him," receiving him "as the judge doth the wrestler after his labors." Henry stresses Christ's initiative — He "sought him, and looked after him, that he might encourage and comfort him," because the man had "so bravely" defended Him. Robertson weighs the manuscripts, preferring "Son of Man" with the older witnesses, while granting either title is "a distinct Messianic claim," and Clarke equates believing on the Son of God with believing on "the Messiah" (Matthew 16:16). Calvin draws the consolation that crowns the scene: the enemies' excommunications are "trivial", for the one they expel is sought and owned by the Son of God Himself.
36:
The man's eager reply — "Who is he, Lord, that I might believe on him?" — the witnesses read as the readiness of a heart already inclined to faith. The grammatical-historical commentators note he does not yet know his questioner. Barnes: "it is probable that the man did not know that he who now addressed him was the same who had healed him," for "he had not yet seen him" (v.7), yet "was prepared to acknowledge him when he did see him." Ellicott and Robertson observe that "Lord" here means no more than "Sir" (as at John 4:11), the reverence due a prophet; he "recognises Him by the voice which he had heard before, and now for the first time sees Him," catching up Christ's words in a final clause, "that I may believe on him."

The Reformed Calvin reads the man as "obedient and ready to receive instruction": the words mean, "As soon as he is pointed out to me, I am ready to embrace him." He "desires to be instructed by Christ as a Prophet," being already convinced Christ "had been sent by God." The Wesleyan Wesley agrees the request "implies some degree of faith already" — "he was ready to receive whatever Jesus said." The witnesses thus find in the question the last step of a steady ascent: from "the man called Jesus" (v.11) to a prophet, to one "of God," the man now stands poised on the threshold of full belief, asking only to be shown its object.

Barnes notes the man "did not know that he who now addressed him was the same who had healed him," never yet having seen Him (v.7), yet "prepared to acknowledge him" at once. Ellicott and Robertson take "Lord" as no more than "Sir" (cf. John 4:11), the man recognising Christ "by the voice" and "now for the first time" seeing Him. Calvin reads him "obedient and ready to receive instruction" — "as soon as he is pointed out to me, I am ready to embrace him" — and Wesley that the request "implies some degree of faith already."
