The witnesses thus find the man poised at the threshold of belief, his ascent from "the man called Jesus" (v.11) needing only its object named.
37:
Christ's self-disclosure — "Thou hast both seen him, and it is he that talketh with thee" — the witnesses set beside His revelation to the Samaritan woman. The grammatical-historical Robertson marks the perfect "thou hast seen him" (since his eyes were opened) and compares John 4:26, "I that speak unto thee am he" — there Jesus reveals Himself as Messiah, here "as the Son of Man." Ellicott finds "a special fitness" in the appeal to sight, "in the case of one whose very power to see was witness to the presence of the Messiah": both the senses now restored to him, hearing and sight, "convey knowledge to the mind."

The Reformed Calvin, characteristically, measures how much — and how little — of faith this disclosure could yet carry: by these words "the blind man could not be carried higher than to a very small and cold portion of faith," for Christ "does not mention his power, or the reason why he was sent... or what he has brought to men." What "principally belongs to faith" — to know that "by the sacrifice of his death, atonement has been made for our sins" — is not yet spoken. The revelation is real but seminal: enough to call forth worship, not yet the full gospel.

The Wesleyan Wesley exclaims at the man's temper — "of so deep and strong an understanding... and yet of so teachable a temper!" The witnesses agree that the same plain honesty which routed the council now makes the man instantly receptive to Christ's word; the One he had defended without seeing, he now both sees and hears, and is ready in the next breath to confess and adore.

Robertson sets the disclosure beside Christ's word to the Samaritan woman, "I that speak unto thee am he" (John 4:26), here revealing Himself "as the Son of Man." Ellicott finds a peculiar fitness in appealing to the man's sight, since "his very power to see was witness to the presence of the Messiah," both restored senses now bearing knowledge to his mind. Calvin, ever weighing degrees of faith, cautions that the man is "carried" only to "a very small and cold portion," Christ not yet naming His "power, or the reason why he was sent," nor the atonement that "principally belongs to faith." Wesley admires the convert's temper — "of so deep and strong an understanding... and yet of so teachable a temper!" The One he defended unseen he now sees and hears, and is ready to adore.
38:
The climax — "Lord, I believe. And he worshipped him" — the witnesses read as the consummation of the man's spiritual journey and a witness to the worship due Christ. The grammatical-historical Ellicott traces the ascent: "his half-faith has passed into full conviction," the "spiritual education" leading him step by step "from 'the Man that is called Jesus'" (v.11) to "a prophet" (v.17), to "of God" (v.33), and now to the Messiah confessed and adored. Robertson stresses the verb "worshipped": a "short creed, but to the point," and the word that in John "is always used to express divine worship" — "it is tragic to hear men today deny that Jesus should be worshipped." Barnes and Clarke agree he "did homage to him as the Messiah," Clarke finding him "convinced of his divinity" and glorifying Him "as his Savior."

The Reformed Calvin weighs the question whether the man "worshipped Christ as God," and answers cautiously: the word denotes "respect and homage" by bending the knee, yet here "something rare and uncommon" — the man "gave far more honor to Christ than to an ordinary man, or even to a prophet." Whether his knowledge of Christ's Godhead was yet full or not, the witnesses agree the act is true worship, the fitting close to a chapter that has moved from physical sight to the sight of faith — and the rebuke, in Robertson's reading, of all who would withhold from Christ the homage this once-blind beggar freely gave.

Ellicott traces the completed ascent — "his half-faith has passed into full conviction," the man led "step by step" from "the Man that is called Jesus" (v.11) to "a prophet" (v.17), to one "of God" (v.33), and now to the Messiah confessed. Robertson calls it "a short creed, but to the point," and presses the verb "worshipped," which in John "is always used to express divine worship" — finding it "tragic" that any should "deny that Jesus should be worshipped." Calvin grants the homage is "rare and uncommon," "far more than to an ordinary man, or even to a prophet," while leaving the fullness of the man's knowledge an open question. The chapter that began with physical sight ends in the sight of faith bowed in worship.
39:
Christ's pronouncement — "For judgment I am come into this world, that they which see not might see; and that they which see might be made blind" — the witnesses read with care, lest "judgment" be flattened into mere condemnation. The grammatical-historical Barnes insists the word cannot mean condemnation, since Christ elsewhere says He "came not to condemn the world" (John 3:17; 12:47); to judge is rather "to express an opinion in a judicial manner." Robertson derives judgement from the verb "to separate," a "sifting" the Father has turned over to the Son (John 5:22), and finds Christ "engaged in that very work by this miracle." Gill notes the saying is no contradiction of John 3:17.

The Reformed Calvin reads "judgment" as including "the grace of illumination": Christ "restores to proper order what was disordered," and this "by a wonderful purpose of God, and contrary to the ordinary opinion of men." The Wesleyan Wesley states the double effect plainly: by Christ's coming, "while the blind in body and soul receive their sight, they who boast they see, will be given up to still greater blindness." Clarke applies it historically — sight to "the Gentiles who sit in darkness," blindness to those who "abuse the mercy of God." The healed beggar and the seeing Pharisees stand before us as the two outcomes embodied: the blind man sees, and the self-styled seers are blind.

Barnes and Gill guard the word from meaning bare condemnation, since Christ "came not to condemn the world" (John 3:17; 12:47); it means rather to judge in the sense of separating. Robertson derives judgement from "to separate," a "sifting" the Father has committed to the Son (John 5:22), Christ "engaged in that very work by this miracle." Calvin reads it as the restoring of "order," a judgment that "include[s] the grace of illumination." Wesley states the twofold issue: "the blind in body and soul receive their sight," while "they who boast they see" are given to "greater blindness." The healed beggar and the seeing rulers stand as the two outcomes embodied — the very sifting Christ describes is enacted before our eyes.
40:
The Pharisees' stung question — "Are we blind also?" — the witnesses read as conscience pricked. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the form expects the answer no, "and yet these Pharisees who overheard the words of Jesus... vaguely suspected that Jesus was referring to them by the last clause" — recalling that in Galilee He had called the Pharisees "blind guides" who "stumble into the pit" (Matthew 15:14). Ellicott observes it is the presence of "mental conditions such as theirs and such as his" — the proud who claim to see, the humble lately blind — that has called forth Christ's saying, and that some had "probably followed the beggar," hoping for "a fresh charge."

The Reformed Calvin reads them "instantly perceiv[ing] that they were smitten," yet judges they were not of the "worst class," for the open enemies "did not at all associate with him," while these "submitted to listen to Christ." Still it profited them nothing, "for no man is qualified to be a disciple of Christ, until he has been divested of self," and they were "very far from being so." Their question, born of "indignation," betrays the very self-confidence that blinds them: they cannot conceive that they, the teachers, might be the unseeing — which is precisely Christ's point in the answer to come.

Robertson notes the form of the question expects the answer no, yet the Pharisees "vaguely suspected that Jesus was referring to them," recalling His charge elsewhere that they were "blind guides" who stumble into the pit (Matthew 15:14). Ellicott supposes some had "followed the beggar," hoping the interview would yield "a fresh charge." Calvin reads them "smitten" by the saying, yet not of the "worst class," since the open enemies "did not at all associate with him" while these "submitted to listen" — though to no profit, "for no man is qualified to be a disciple of Christ, until he has been divested of self." Their indignant question betrays the very self-assurance that blinds them; that the teachers might be the unseeing is a thought they cannot entertain.
41:
Christ's closing word — "If ye were blind, ye should have no sin: but now ye say, We see; therefore your sin remaineth" — the witnesses read as the chapter's verdict on willful blindness. The grammatical-historical Barnes states the principle: "Sin is measured by the capacities or ability of men, and by their opportunities of knowing the truth"; had they been truly ignorant and "willing to confess it," they "would not be guilty," but having "all proper ability, and no disposition," God holds them to blame. Robertson parses the second-class condition ("if ye were blind" — but ye are not), so that, were they "born morally blind," they would be "without responsibility"; their claim to sight is their condemnation. Clarke and Ellicott agree: their boast "we see" — that they are "perfectly capable of judging" — fixes the guilt, for theirs is an ignorance "not... willing to be overcome."

The Reformed Calvin offers two readings — that ignorance "would, in some degree, alleviate their guilt" were they not "deliberately fight[ing] against the truth" (so John 15:22), or that their "disease of ignorance might be cured" would they only "acknowledge it." The Wesleyan Wesley sums the antithesis: "If ye had been" invincibly ignorant, "ye would have had no sin"; "but now ye say, Ye see... therefore your sin remaineth" — "without excuse, without remedy." The man born blind has received both kinds of sight; the rulers, claiming sight, have lost it — and on that solemn reversal the chapter closes.

Barnes states the governing principle: "Sin is measured by the capacities or ability of men, and by their opportunities of knowing the truth" — true ignorance, humbly owned, "would not be guilty," but ability joined to "no disposition" incurs blame. Robertson parses the second-class condition ("if ye were blind" — but you are not), so that real, congenital blindness would leave them "without responsibility," while their boast convicts them. Calvin offers his two readings — ignorance might "alleviate their guilt" were they not "deliberately fight[ing] against the truth" (John 15:22), or might be "cured" would they "acknowledge it." Wesley sums the verdict: claiming sight, they are left "without excuse, without remedy." The man born blind has gained both sights; the seers, by claiming sight, have forfeited it.
